posts.title: Getting Started with the Next.js App Router
posts.excerpt: The App Router is the modern way to build Next.js applications. In this post we cover layouts, loading states, and error boundaries from scratch.
posts.content:
## What is the App Router?

The App Router was introduced in Next.js 13 and became stable in Next.js 14. It replaces the older Pages Router with a file-system based routing model built on React Server Components.

## Layouts

Every `layout.tsx` file wraps every page below it in the folder tree. The root layout is mandatory and must include `<html>` and `<body>` tags.

## Loading States

Add a `loading.tsx` file next to any `page.tsx` and Next.js will automatically show it while the page fetches data.

## Error Boundaries

Add an `error.tsx` file to catch runtime errors in a route segment without crashing the whole app.

posts.title: Server Actions Explained: No More API Routes
posts.excerpt: Server Actions let you run server-side code directly from your components. Here is everything you need to know to replace your API routes.
posts.content:
## What are Server Actions?

Server Actions are async functions that run on the server. You can call them directly from client or server components without writing a separate API route.

## Basic Example

```ts
'use server'

export async function createPost(formData: FormData) {
  const title = formData.get('title') as string
  await db.post.create({ data: { title } })
  revalidatePath('/blog')
}
```

## When to still use Route Handlers

Use Route Handlers for webhooks, third-party callbacks, or when you need full HTTP control. For everything else, Server Actions are simpler.

posts.title: TypeScript Generics: A Practical Guide
posts.excerpt: Generics are one of the most powerful TypeScript features. This guide walks through real use cases you will encounter every day.
posts.content:
## Why Generics?

Without generics you end up writing the same logic for different types, or you lose type safety by using `any`. Generics solve both problems.

## A Simple Generic Function

```ts
function getFirst<T>(arr: T[]): T {
  return arr[0]
}

const first = getFirst<string>(['a', 'b', 'c']) // type: string
```

## Generic Constraints

```ts
function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] {
  return obj[key]
}
```

posts.title: CSS Container Queries Are Here
posts.excerpt: Container queries let components respond to their parent size instead of the viewport. This changes how we think about responsive design.

posts.title: How I Got My First Freelance Client as a Developer
posts.excerpt: Landing the first client is the hardest part. Here is the exact approach I used: no cold emails, no job boards, just one focused strategy.
posts.content:
## Stop Applying, Start Showing

Most developers look for clients the wrong way. They send cold emails and browse job boards. The approach that worked for me was simpler: build one thing publicly and let clients come to you.

## What I Built

I created a restaurant menu management demo — a real working app, not a mockup. I posted it in a local business Facebook group with a simple message: 'I built this for a restaurant. If you want something similar, message me.'

## The Result

Three restaurant owners messaged within a week. I closed one project that same month.

posts.title: React useMemo and useCallback: When to Actually Use Them
posts.excerpt: Most developers overuse useMemo and useCallback. This post explains when they actually help and when they just add noise.
posts.content:
## The Misconception

`useMemo` and `useCallback` do not make your app faster by default. They add overhead. The question is whether that overhead is worth the optimization.

## When useMemo Helps

Use `useMemo` when the computation is genuinely expensive and the dependencies change infrequently.

```tsx
const sortedList = useMemo(() => {
  return items.sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name))
}, [items])
```

## When to Skip It

If the computation is simple, the memoization cost exceeds the savings. Just compute it inline.

comments.body: Great intro! The part about loading.tsx saved me a lot of confusion. I had been manually tracking loading state in useState.
Does this work the same way for the Pages Router? I am still on an older project.
Hi Lena — no, loading.tsx is App Router only. In the Pages Router you would handle loading state manually with router events.
This finally clicked for me. I had been creating an API route for every single form. Server Actions are so much cleaner.
The Facebook group idea is underrated. Most devs ignore local communities completely.
